feed.title: Combined summary - SHRINCS: 324-byte stateful post-quantum signatures with static backups

entry.title: Combined summary - SHRINCS: 324-byte stateful post-quantum signatures with static backups
entry.summary: SHRINCS is a hybrid signature scheme that merges the efficiency of stateful hash-based signatures with the robustness of stateless ones. This dual approach allows for extremely efficient operations under normal conditions while providing a reliable fallback if the state is lost or corrupted. The public key in SHRINCS combines both stateful and stateless keys through hashing, facilitating seamless operation between modes depending on the state’s integrity. Key operations such as generation, restoration, signing, and verification are all supported by this dual-mode functionality. The initial setup of SHRINCS utilizes a master seed to generate secret keys for both the stateful and stateless schemes, deriving public keys accordingly. If the state remains intact, SHRINCS employs a more efficient stateful signing mechanism. However, should the state be considered lost—for instance, after a restore from a static seed—the system defaults to the stateless method. The stateful component features an "unbalanced" version of the XMSS tree, optimized to reduce authentication path length for early signatures which is crucial for applications expecting fewer signatures, like Bitcoin wallets. Moreover, the use of WOTS+C within the stateful path enhances both efficiency and security, despite the inherent risks linked to managing the state securely. In cases of uncertainty about the state's integrity, SHRINCS can seamlessly transition to stateless signatures, ensuring continued security. For further details on SHRINCS, including its mathematical formulations and potential applications, one can refer to the paper titled “Hash-based Signatures for Bitcoin,” co-authored by Mikhail Kudinov, available at [https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/2203](https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/2203). The discourse also extends into post-quantum cryptography (PQC) concerns, particularly around the adequacy of a 128-bit security level in the face of quantum computational attacks, such as those facilitated by Grover's algorithm. It is highlighted that a 128-bit key effectively provides a 64-bit security level against these attacks, assuming each byte is accounted for in the calculation. This revelation underscores the need for ongoing reassessment of cryptographic strength not just through conventional metrics but also via quantum-resistant measures. In addition, detailed discussions focus on the intricacies of cryptographic security concerning the Winternitz One-Time Signature Scheme (WOTS) and its enhanced variant WOTS+. A significant point of interest here is the role of checksums in preventing specific types of attacks. The checksum mechanism ensures that any attempt to modify and impersonate the original message during the verification process is thwarted, maintaining the authenticity and reliability of communications. Understanding these complex layers of cryptographic technology and its implications on security, especially in blockchain applications, highlights the continual evolution needed in cryptographic practices as digital security landscapes shift toward anticipating advancements in quantum computing.
